coredata.dc:title: Preparation of salicylic acid nano-protectant with dual synergistic mechanism: High direct fungicidal activity and plant defence toward cotton Verticillium wilt

coredata.dc:description: Nanomaterials exhibit a wide range of potential applications in agricultural field. Here we construct a series of nano-protectants based on one model plant elicitor salicylic acid (SA) and six types of nanomaterials, including mesoporous silica nanosphere (MSN), mesoporous Fe3O4 nanosphere (Fe3O4), carbon quantum dot (CQD), polylactic acid-glycolic acid copolymer (PLGA), star polycation (SPc) and hydrophilic and lipophilic diblock polymer (HLDP), and explore the best nanomaterial for plant disease control. Our results demonstrate that the HLDP-SA nano-protectant displays the best control effects on cotton Verticillium wilt caused by one of the most notorious soil-borne fungi (Verticillium dahliae). The HLDP self-assembles with SA via electrostatic interaction into HLDP-SA nano-protectant, which changes the morphology of SA from needle-like particles to spherical particles. The loading efficiency of HLDP toward SA is calculated to be 49.28%, and the plant uptake of HLDP-loaded SA significantly increases by 3–4 times. HLDP-SA nano-protectant can suppress the spore germination of V991 strain with the spore germination rate of merely 2%, and its colony diameter is significantly decreased by 64.9%. Our transcriptome data demonstrate that the HLDP-SA nano-protectant accelerates the delivery and translocation of exogenous substances into V. dahliae, which exhibits the highest direct fungicidal activity via inhibiting the metabolism and weakening the pathogenicity. Meanwhile, its enhanced plant uptake with better adhesion performance on leaves amplifies the plant defense induced by SA via accelerating the biosynthesis of plant secondary metabolites, such as SA, abscisic acid (ABA) and gossypol. This study provides a powerful nanomaterial with dual synergistic mechanism for designing nano-protectant with high fungicidal activity and amplified plant defense.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization and properties of SA nano-protectants. a) Morphologies and particle size distributions of MSN-SA, Fe3O4-SA, PLGA-SA, CQD-SA, SPc-SA and HLDP-SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL). The particle size was measured from three independent samples. b) Zeta potentials of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL). Different letters indicate significant differences according to the Tukey HSD test (P<0.05). Each treatment contained three independent samples. c) Particle sizes and zeta potentials of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL) after the storage for stability test. Each treatment included three independent samples. d) Contact angles of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL) on cotton leaves. The 5 μL of each formulation was dripped onto the leaf, and the image was collected. The ddH2O was tested as control, and the test was repeated four times for each solution. e) Retention of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL) on cotton leaves. Each treatment included 8 independent samples. f) Plant uptake of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL). Each treatment contained three independent samples.

Direct fungicidal activities of SA nano-protectants. a) Spore germination rates of V. dahliae incubated with SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL). The data were calculated from three independent samples at 12 and 24 h after the incubation. The ddH2O was tested as control. b) Colony diameters of V. dahliae inoculated on PDA medium containing SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL) for 14 d. The data were recorded from three independent samples using cross method. Different letters indicate significant differences according to the Tukey HSD test (P<0.05).

Plant defense induced by SA nano-protectants toward Verticillium wilt. a) Control effects of SA nano-protectants on Verticillium wilt. The potted cotton seedlings were sprayed with SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL, 25 mL per plant) and then inoculated with V. dahliae. The disease index was calculated at 22 d after the inoculation. Each group contained ten plants, and the experiment was repeated three times. Fungal biomass was quantified in cotton roots, and the expression level of target gene (Z29511) was normalized to the abundance of cotton housekeeping gene Ployubiquitin. Each treatment comprised three independent samples. Different letters indicate significant differences according to the Tukey HSD test (P<0.05). b) Oxidative stress response in cotton seedlings treated with SA nano-protectants. The 0.1 g leaves were used to determine the MDA content, SOD activity, POD activity and CAT activity. Each treatment comprised three independent samples. c) Osmotic adjustment substance in cotton seedlings treated with SA nano-protectants. The 0.2 g leaves were used to determine the contents of soluble protein and proline. Each treatment comprised three independent samples.

RNA-seq analysis to illustrate the potential mechanism underlying fungicidal activity of HLDP-SA nano-protectant. a) Analysis of DEGs in V. dahliae treated with SA and HLDP-SA nano-protectant with the volcano plot. Up- and down-regulated genes are represented by red and blue dots, respectively. b-c) GO and KEGG enrichments of DEGs. d) Heatmaps of various crucial genes related to direct fungicidal activity of HLDP-SA nano-protectant. Each treatment included three independent samples. Highly and lowly expressed genes are labeled as red and blue, respectively.

Conjoint analysis of metabonomics and transcriptomics for amplified plant defense induced by HLDP-SA nano-protectant. a) Analysis of DAMs in cotton seedlings treated with SA and HLDP-SA nano-protectant with the volcano plot. Up- and down-regulated metabolites are represented by red and blue dots, respectively. b) KEGG enrichment of DAMs. c) Common pathways in the metabonomics and transcriptomics. Typical common pathways are shown below. d) Impacts of HLDP-SA nano-protectant on SA biosynthesis. The expression levels of DAMs are indicated by the heatmap using log2(Fold change). Up-regulated DEGs are shown in red. aroC: Branching acid synthase; ICS: isochorismate synthase; PAL: phenylalanine ammonia-lyase; EDS5: enhanced disease susceptibility 5. e) Impacts of HLDP-SA nano-protectant on ABA biosynthesis. NCED: 9-cis-epoxycarotenoid dioxygenase; ZEP: zeaxanthin epoxidase. f) Impacts of HLDP-SA nano-protectant on gossypol biosynthesis. HMGR: HMG-CoA reductase; MVP: phosphomevalonate kinase; CDNS: (+)-δ-cadinene synthase; CYP82D113: cytochrome P450 monooxygenases; 2-ODD-1: 2-oxoglutarate/Fe (II) dependent dioxygenase.

head.ce:title: Preparation of salicylic acid nano-protectant with dual synergistic mechanism: High direct fungicidal activity and plant defence toward cotton Verticillium wilt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nanomaterials exhibit a wide range of potential applications in agricultural field. Here we construct a series of nano-protectants based on one model plant elicitor salicylic acid (SA) and six types of nanomaterials, including mesoporous silica nanosphere (MSN), mesoporous Fe3O4 nanosphere (Fe3O4), carbon quantum dot (CQD), polylactic acid-glycolic acid copolymer (PLGA), star polycation (SPc) and hydrophilic and lipophilic diblock polymer (HLDP), and explore the best nanomaterial for plant disease control. Our results demonstrate that the HLDP-SA nano-protectant displays the best control effects on cotton Verticillium wilt caused by one of the most notorious soil-borne fungi (Verticillium dahliae). The HLDP self-assembles with SA via electrostatic interaction into HLDP-SA nano-protectant, which changes the morphology of SA from needle-like particles to spherical particles. The loading efficiency of HLDP toward SA is calculated to be 49.28%, and the plant uptake of HLDP-loaded SA significantly increases by 3–4 times. HLDP-SA nano-protectant can suppress the spore germination of V991 strain with the spore germination rate of merely 2%, and its colony diameter is significantly decreased by 64.9%. Our transcriptome data demonstrate that the HLDP-SA nano-protectant accelerates the delivery and translocation of exogenous substances into V. dahliae, which exhibits the highest direct fungicidal activity via inhibiting the metabolism and weakening the pathogenicity. Meanwhile, its enhanced plant uptake with better adhesion performance on leaves amplifies the plant defense induced by SA via accelerating the biosynthesis of plant secondary metabolites, such as SA, abscisic acid (ABA) and gossypol. This study provides a powerful nanomaterial with dual synergistic mechanism for designing nano-protectant with high fungicidal activity and amplified plant defense.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Nano-enabled strategies exhibit a wide range of potential applications in pharmaceutical and agricultural fields, which have revolutionized the design and development of novel drugs [1,2,3]. Some kinds of nanomaterials display specific antimicrobial and insecticidal activities via damaging cell membrane and elevating cellular reactive oxygen species (ROS) level, which can be applied directly as fungicidal and insecticidal active ingredients (AIs) [4,5,6]. Traditional fungicides exhibit poor dispersibility in aqueous solutions, limiting their bioactivity and posing environmental risks in actual production. Nanomaterial has shown enormous potential in delivering various fungicides [7]. Nanomaterials can act as adjuvants to improve the physicochemical properties of fungicides and deliver them into target harmful organisms [8–14]. Thus, the bioactivity improvement of nano-enabled fungicides is derived from the direct activity of nanomaterials and nanomaterial-mediated enhanced delivery of AIs.

In recent years, various types of nanomaterials such as inorganic, organic and composite nanomaterials have been applied to prepare nano-enabled pesticides, and the overall efficacy is 31.5 % higher than conventional pesticides, including an 18.9 % increased efficacy in the field trials [15]. However, the synergistic performance and mechanism obviously vary among different types of nanomaterials [16,17]. The adhesion performance of nano-enabled pesticides on the surfaces of target organisms and leaves is one of the most important indicators for evaluating the synergistic performance, which directly influences the fragmentation, splashing, bouncing and rolling of nano-pesticides [18]. Many complicated parameters can affect the adhesion performance of nano-pesticides [19,20]. For instance, the functional groups of nanomaterials may help to decrease the surface tension and contact angle of nano-enabled pesticides on plant leaves, and their positive charge can improve the affinity with leaves, which are beneficial for promoting the spreading of droplets [21,22]. Furthermore, the particle size of nano-enabled pesticides is another vital parameter, and the smaller size can not only amplify the contact area of nano-pesticides on harmful organisms, but also improve the plant uptake [23,24]. For instance, the adhesion of AuNPs with smaller particle size increases on the leaf surface when wheat leaves are sprayed with the suspension of gold nanoparticles (3, 10 and 50 nm) [25].

A model plant elicitor salicylic acid (SA) is a perfect AI to test best nanomaterial for plant protection. As a typical signalling molecule, the SA should be taken up by plants to induce the broad-spectrum resistance to various pathogenic microorganisms such as fungi, bacteria and viruses [26]. The SA can up-regulate the expression of antimicrobial pathogenesis-related (PR) genes to inhibit pathogen growth and spread, which can be applied to induce the plant defense in cotton, tobacco, rice, tomato, etc. [27–31]. Furthermore, the SA also displays antifungal activity toward various pathogens, which mainly reduces the pathogenicity by inhibiting the activity of plant cell wall degrading enzymes secreted by pathogens [26,32,33]. Herein, a series of SA nano-protectants were prepared using four commercial and two novel nanomaterials developed by our group, including the mesoporous silica nanosphere (MSN), mesoporous Fe3O4 nanosphere (Fe3O4), carbon quantum dot (CQD), polylactic acid-glycolic acid copolymer (PLGA), star polycation (SPc) and hydrophilic and lipophilic diblock polymer (HLDP). To screen the best nanomaterial for designing nano-protectants, the control effects of various SA nano-protectants were determined toward Verticillium dahliae, which is one of the most notorious soil-borne fungi and causes vascular wilt disease in more than 400 plant species [34,35].

In the current study, the loading efficiencies of six nanocarriers toward SA were firstly determined, and the self-assembly mechanism of SA nano-protectants was analyzed. Then six types of SA nano-protectants were prepared according to the loading efficiencies, and some characterization parameters were adopted to examine their properties. Specifically, the particle sizes and morphologies of SA nano-protectants were examined. The contact angles and retentions of SA nano-protectants on cotton leaves were tested, and their plant uptakes were also investigated. Subsequently, the direct fungicidal activities of SA nano-protectants were tested toward V. dahliae, and their control effects on Verticillium wilt were also evaluated using whole cotton seedlings. As important indicators for plant defense, several parameters such as the activities of superoxide dismutase (SOD), peroxidase (POD) and catalase (CAT) and contents of malondialdehyde (MDA), proline and soluble protein were also examined. Finally, the HLDP-SA nano-protectant exhibited the best performance according to above results. Its direct fungicidal activity was analyzed using RNA sequencing (RNA-seq) and quantitative real-time polymerase chain reaction (qRT-PCR), and the amplified plant defense induced by HLDP-SA nano-protectant was analyzed using metabolome sequencing and RNA-seq. Furthermore, the main indicators for plant defense such as SA, abscisic acid (ABA) and gossypol contents were also quantified to verify the metabolite data. To our knowledge, it is the first time to compare the direct fungicidal activity and delivery efficiency of nanomaterials, which synthesized a high-efficiency nanomaterial for designing and developing fungicides.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental section
Chemical reagents
ce:section.ce:para: The standards of SA, ABA and gossypol were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA). Three types of commercial nanocarriers including MSN, Fe3O4 and COD were bought from Feynman nanomaterials (China), and commercial PLGA was obtained from Shanghai Maclean Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (China). Two-bromo-2-methylpropionyl bromide (BIBB), trimethylamine (TEA, 99 %), one-butanol, ε-caprolactone (ε-CL) and Sn(Oct)2 purchased from Heowns BioChem Technologies (China), 2-(Dimethyl amino) ethyl methacrylate (DMAEMA, 99 %) purchased from Energy Chemical (China), and N,N,N′,N′,N″-Pentamethyl diethylenetriamine (PMDETA, 98 %) and CuBr (99.999 %) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (USA) were used to synthesize SPc and HLDP. Other chemical reagents were obtained from Beijing Chemical Works (China).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Nanocarrier synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: The SPc was synthesized according to the procedure described by Li et al. [36], which is shown in Supplementary Fig. 1a. Pentaerythritol solution (25 g, 0.18 mmol) was added to dry tetrahydrofuran (THF, 20 mL) along with triethylamine (TEA, 111.3 mg, 721.42 mmol) under stirring in an ice bath. Two-bromo-2-methylpropanoyl bromide (253 mg, 1.11 mmol) was added dropwise in portions. Then, methanol was added to quench the reaction. Subsequently, the solvent was extracted with dichloromethane (DCM) and water. The DCM phase was collected, evaporated, and then crystallized at −28 °C. The obtained product was washed with ice-cold acetone to obtain white powdered Pt-Br. DMAEMA (2.2 g, 7.7 mmol) was mixed with Pt-Br in an oil bath at 60 °C for 7 h with PMDETA (110 mg, 0.44 mmol), and CuBr (46 mg, 0.22 mmol) and THF (8 mL) were then added. Finally, the crude polymer was dialyzed in water, and the SPc was obtained as a white powder after freeze-drying.

The HLDP was synthesized similarly to SPc (Supplementary Fig. 1b). One-butanol (100 mg, 1.35 mmol), ε-CL (6.16 g, 54 mmol) and Sn(Oct)2 (310 mg) were incubated in an oil bath at 90 °C for 9 h. DCM (30 mL) was added into the mixture, and the mixture was deposited in excess cold methanol, filtrated and harvested to obtain the final product PCL. BIBB (3 g, 13.04 mmol), THF (30 mL) and TEA (4 g, 40 mmol) were added dropwise into the PCL solution (3 g, 0.58 mmol). Then, methanol was added to quench the reaction. The precipitation was collected by filtration, and the product PCL-Br was obtained as a white powder. PCL-Br (100 mg, 0.04 mmol) and DMAEMA (0.87 g, 6.07 mmol) were dissolved in THF (2 mL) and CuBr (15 mg, 0.1 mmol). PMDETA (37 mg, 0.21 mmol) was then added into the solution, and the polymerization was carried out in an oil bath at 65 °C. When the reaction was stopped, the reaction system was treated with a liquid nitrogen bath and then heated to room temperature. Then, the HLDP in the form of white powder was obtained after the dialysis in water and freeze-drying.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading capacity assay toward SA
ce:section.ce:para: The SA standard was dissolved in methanol/ddH2O (1: 19 v: v) to prepare a series of SA diluents (25, 50, 60, 80 and 100 μg/mL), and the peak area of each sample was determined using HPLC (Agilent, Germany). The liquid mobile phase was methanol: 0.5 % acetic acid water (6:4 v/v), and the flow rate was 1.0 mL/min. The column temperature was 30 °C. The detection wavelength of SA was 306 nm. The SA concentration was proportional to the peak area, which was used to construct a standard calibration curve.

The SA solution (1 mg/mL) was individually incubated with each nanocarrier aqueous solution (1 mg/mL) with the same volume for 30 min. Above mixtures were dialyzed using the dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off value of 1000 Da (Shanghai Yuanye Biotechnology Co., Ltd., China). The dialysate was subjected to freeze-drying and subsequently weighed. Methanol/ddH2O (1: 19 v/v, 5 mL) was added to dissolve the freeze-dried solid. HPLC was used to determine the SA content in dialysate. Each treatment included three independent samples. The SA loading efficiency was calculated using the equation of SA loading efficiency (%) = weight of SA loaded in nano-protectant ÷ weight of nano-protectant × 100. A series of SA nano-protectants were prepared according to SA loading efficiencies in the following experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) assay
ce:section.ce:para: The dominant interaction forces between SA and nanocarriers (SPc and HLDP) were investigated using the widely adopted ITC method to illustrate the self-assembly mechanism of SA nano-protectants. The 300 μL solution of SA (1 mM) was titrated with 40 μL nanocarrier aqueous solution (1 mM) in Nano ITC (TA Instruments Waters, USA) at 25 °C. During each injection, the heating temperature of the interaction was calculated by integrating each titration peak via Origin7 software (OriginLab Co., Ltd., USA), and ΔG value was calculated using the formula of ΔG=ΔH – TΔS.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Particle size measurement and morphology observation of SA nano-protectant
ce:section.ce:para: The particle sizes and zeta-potentials of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL) and nanocarrier alone (1 mg/mL) were investigated using Zeta Sizer Nano Series (Malvern Instruments, UK). Each treatment comprised three independent samples. The morphologies of above samples and SA standard were characterized using both scanning electron microscope (SEM, JSM-6700F, Japan) and transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2100F, Japan). SEM sample preparation: each sample was dropped onto the silicon wafer, and left to dry naturally at 25 °C. Gold was sprayed onto the sample for testing. TEM sample preparation: each sample was dropped onto the copper net, and then observed after the air drying. For the stability text of various SA nano-protectants, their particle sizes and zeta-potentials were measured at 1, 3, 7, 30, 60, 90 and 120 d after the storage.

To analyze SA nano-protectant dissolution and release of SA from SA nano-protectants, nano-protectants were dissolved in ultrapure deionized water under oscillation conditions. The particle size was measured at 1, 2 and 3 d to explore the relationship between the nano-protectant dissolution and particle size. For SA release, SA nano-protectants were dissolved in ultrapure deionized water, collected under oscillation conditions at 3 and 6h, and used to determine the SA content using HPLC, following the above method. Each treatment included three independent samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Contact angle and retention analysis of SA nano-protectant
ce:section.ce:para: Contact angle and retention of SA nano-protectants were evaluated using the cotton leaves. The Coker 312 was employed as the tested cotton variety. Cotton seeds were disinfected in 0.1 % sodium hypochlorite solution for 15 min, and then washed with ddH2O. After soaking in ddH2O for 24 h, the seeds were sown on damp filter paper, and incubated at 28 °C for 72 h. Then, the seeds were sown in the soil substrate (peat: vermiculite = 1:2) under suitable growth conditions (28 °C, 16/8h light/dark and 70 % relative humidity).

Fresh and intact cotton leaves were cut and laid flat on glass slides. The 5 μL solutions of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL), nanocarrier alone and SA standard were vertically dripped onto the leaves using a contact angle measurement analyzer (Dataphysics OCA50AF, Germany), waited for 10 s for droplet stability, and took photos. The elliptical fitting algorithm was used to calculate the contact angle of the droplet image. The ddH2O was tested as control, and the test was repeated four times for each solution.

Leaves (0.8 cm2) were collected, weighted, and then completely immersed in various solutions of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL), nanocarrier alone and SA standard for 30 s. The treated leaves were removed and weighed until no droplets dropped. The retention was calculated from 8 independent samples using the equation of retention (mg/cm2) = (leaf weight after immersion – leaf weight before immersion) ÷ leaf area.

Plant uptake analysis of SA nano-protectant: The cotton seedlings with the same growth status (two leaves and one heart) were washed with ddH2O, and then dried naturally with clean absorbent paper. The seedlings were immersed in the solutions of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL) and SA standard for 1 min. The residual chemicals on seedlings were cleaned with ddH2O at 6 h after the immersion, and the roots, stems and leaves were individually harvested for determining the plant uptake of SA. Each treatment included three independent samples.

The SA was extracted from roots, stems and leaves (5 g) using 20 mL methanol. After the centrifugation, 5 mL supernatant was evaporated using nitrogen (40 °C), and then dissolved in methanol/0.1 % acetic acid water (2:8 v/v) to a final volume of 1 mL. Each sample was purified using PTFE filter membrane (Haiming Zhongli Filtering Equipment Factory, China) for liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (LC−MS/MS) analysis, which was performed on an ACQUITY UPLC-TQD system with an ACQUITY UPLC BEH C18 column (Waters Co., USA). Two analytes were separated using a mobile phase consisting of 0.1 % acetic acid water and methanol (20:80 v/v). The injection volume was 20 μL per sample, and the column temperature was set at 40 °C. Other operating conditions: 4000 V capillary voltage, 0.4 MPa nebulizer pressure, and 200 μL/min flow rate of the mobile phase. Each sample was tested three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fungicidal activity assay of SA nano-protectant toward v. Dahliae
ce:section.ce:para: The impacts of various SA nano-protectants on the spore germination and growth of V. dahliae strain (V991) were evaluated to assess the direct fungicidal activities. The spore suspension of V991 strain (1 mL) was adjusted to 2 × 107 cfu/mL, which was individually incubated with the same volume of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL) and nanocarrier alone on a concave glass slide. The spore germination rate was calculated from three independent samples at 12 and 24 h after the incubation using a microscope. The ddH2O (control) and SA standard were also tested.

Spore suspension (200 μL) was uniformly spread on potato dextrose agar (PDA) medium. After 7 d of 25 °C dark culture, the colonies (5 mm) were inoculated on PDA medium containing SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL), nanocarriers and SA standard with a punch. The ddH2O was used as control. The growth of V991 strain was observed at 14 d after the inoculation, and the colony diameter was recorded from three independent samples using cross method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Control effect assay of SA nano-protectant toward Verticillium wilt
ce:section.ce:para: As a mid to early maturing cotton variety, the Coker 312 is highly susceptible to V. dahliae, which was used to evaluate the control effects of various SA nano-protectants. The solutions of SA nano-protectants (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL), SA standard and ddH2O (25 mL per plant) were sprayed on the cotton seedlings with 4–5 real leaves. Root injury inoculation method was carried out at 24 h after the spraying. Seedlings were uprooted, and the roots were submerged in a spore suspension (1 × 107 cfu/mL). The seedlings were replanted and grown for 22 d, and the disease severity was extensively evaluated using multiple parameters.

Disease grades were classified according to the method described by Xu et al. [37]. Grade 0: no disease; Grade 1: 1–2 cotyledons or true leaves had small part of wilting; Grade 2: 1–2 cotyledons or true leaves had large area of wilting; Grade 3: more than three leaves had large area of wilting, and partial leaves dropped; Grade 4: all leaves had fallen off, and the plant had died. Each group contained ten plants, and the experiment was repeated three times. The disease index (DI) was calculated as follows:
Diseaseindex
( D I )
=
∑
N u m b e r o f d i s e a s e d p l a n t s × g r a d e
Numberoftotalcheckedplants × 4
× 100

Fungal biomass was quantified in cotton roots to investigate the infection severity. Based on the method described by Su et al., genomic DNA was isolated from the roots using a DNAsecure plant kit (TIANGEN), and the fungal biomass was then examined using qRT-PCR [35]. The qRT-PCR was performed with Taq Pro Universal SYBR qPCR Master Mix (Vazyme) using an ABI7500 Fast instrument (Applied Biosystems, USA). The primers are listed in Supplementary Table 1. Reactions were performed in triplicate under the following conditions: one cycle at 95 °C for 10 min, followed by 40 cycles at 95 °C for 15 s, 60 °C for 30 s, and 72 °C for 35 s. The expression level of target gene (Z29511) was normalized to the abundance of cotton housekeeping gene Ployubiquitin using the 2-ΔΔCT method [38]. Each treatment comprised three independent samples.

The changes in protective enzyme activity were investigated in cotton seedlings. Leaves were frozen and grinded into powder. The 0.1 g leaves were used to determine the malondialdehyde (MDA) content, superoxide dismutase (SOD) activity, peroxidase (POD) activity and catalase (CAT) activity using a Solarbio assay kit (Beijing, China) according to the protocol. The 0.2 g leaves were used to determine the contents of soluble protein and proline using a Nanjing Jiancheng Research Institute assay kit (China). Each treatment comprised three independent samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Rna-seq and qRT-PCR for analyzing the direct fungicidal activity of HLDP-SA nano-protectant
ce:section.ce:para: The V991 strain was incubated on PDA solid culture medium containing HLDP-SA nano-protectant (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL) and SA standard at 25℃ for 14 d. The mycelium was then scraped from the glass paper on the surface of PDA medium. Total RNA was extracted using an EasyPure® RNA kit (TransGen Biotech Co., China) for RNA-seq. Each treatment comprised three independent samples. After the sample testing, library construction, and quality testing, the Illumina Novaseq 6000 high-throughput sequencing platform (Illumina, USA) was used for sequencing. Trimmomatic software (v0.30) (Del Lab, USA) was used to delete the adapter sequences and low-quality bases (<Q20). The mRNA seq data were pre-treated using Bowtie 2 (2.1.0) software (Institut Français de Bioinformatique, France). V. dahliae (ASM15067v2) reference genome was adopted to achieve the sequence alignment of clean paired-end reads. The gene expression level was expressed as Fragments Per Kilobase of transcript per Million mapped reads (FPKM). DESeq was used to analyze the DEGs, and FDR<0.01 and fold change ≥1.5 were the screen conditions. Gene ontology (GO) and Kyoto Encyclopedia of Genes and Genomes (KEGG) enrichment analysis were carried out for gene enrichment and functional annotation.

The above RNA samples were reverse transcribed using a ReverTra Ace ® QPCR RT Master Mix with gDNA Remover kit (Toyobo, FSQ-301, China). The qRT-PCR was performed to verify the expression levels of target genes using GAPDH as reference gene. Each treatment comprised three independent samples.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Metabolomics and RNA-seq for analyzing the amplified plant defense induced by HLDP-SA nano-protectant
ce:section.ce:para: The solution of HLDP-SA nano-protectant (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL; 25 mL per plant) was sprayed on the cotton seedlings. Root injury inoculation method was carried out at 24 h after the spraying. Seedlings were treated with the spore suspension (1 × 107 cfu/mL) and grown for 7 d, and the samples were harvested for testing. Freeze-dried seedlings were grinded using a mixer mill (MM 400, Germany) at 30 Hz for 1.5 min. One hundred mg of sample powder was used for the extraction overnight with 70 % methanol aqueous solution containing 0.1 mg/L lidocaine at 4℃. After the centrifugation, the supernatant was harvested and filtered using a filter with a diameter of 0.22 μm, followed by Liquid Chromatograph Mass Spectrometer (LC-MS/MS) analysis. The pretreatment of LC/MS raw data was performed by Progenesis QI software (Waters Corporation, USA). Then, the metabolites with variable importance in the projeciton (VIP) > 1 and P<0.05 were determined as differential metabolites, which was obtained by OPLS-DA model and P-value generated by student’s t test. Differential metabolites among two groups were mapped into their biochemical pathways through metabolic enrichment and pathway analysis according to KEGG database (https://www. genome.jp/kegg/). Each treatment included six independent samples.

Above treated seedlings were harvested for RNA-seq similarly as above experiment for fungus. Each treatment comprised three independent samples. The cotton samples were subjected to a combined analysis of transcriptomics and metabolomics.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Content determination of plant secondary metabolites related with plant defense
ce:section.ce:para: The solution of HLDP-SA nano-protectant (SA concentration: 1 mg/mL; 25 mL per plant) was sprayed on the cotton seedlings. Seedlings were treated with the spore suspension (1 × 107 cfu/mL) at 24 h after the spraying, grown for 7 d, and harvested for testing. The contents of SA, ABA and gossypol in seedlings were measured. Each treatment included three independent samples. The cotton tissue (0.5 g) was fully grinded with liquid nitrogen, and added with 5 mL isopropanol/hydrochloric acid buffer solution. The mixture was added with dichloromethane (10 mL) and shaken for 30 min. The lower organic phase was centrifuged, dried with nitrogen gas, and dissolved in 500 μL methanol formic acid solution. The contents of SA and ABA were determined by HPLC. A series of ABA standard solution (25, 50, 60, 80 and 100 μg/mL) were prepared, and HPLC was used to determine the peak area of each sample. The ABA concentration was proportional to the peak area, and the standard calibration curve of ABA was constructed by measuring the peak area of the standard sample. The liquid mobile phase was methanol: 0.5 % acetic acid water (6:4 v/v), and the flow rate was 1.0 mL/min. The column temperature was 30 °C. The detection wavelengths of SA and ABA were 306 nm and 260 nm, respectively. Each sample was tested three times.

The gossypol content was quantified according to the method described by Li et al. [39]. The cotton seedling was firstly dried in a 65℃ oven, and the seedlings (0.1 g) were grinded into powder. Acetone (5 mL) was then added for ultrasonic extraction, and the volume was fixed to a 25 mL brown volumetric flask. A series of gossypol standard solutions (25, 50, 75, 100 and 150 μg/mL) were prepared, and their peak areas were determined using HPLC to construct a standard calibration curve. The chromatographic conditions were as follows: chromatographic column C18, mobile phase: methanol and 0.2 % phosphoric acid (9:1 v/v) solution, flow rate of 1.0 mL/min, detection wavelength of 238 nm, injection volume of 10 μL, and column temperature: 28℃. Each sample was tested three times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Safety evaluation of HLDP-SA nano-protectant toward plants
ce:section.ce:para: Coker 312 and nipponbare were used to evaluate the potential negative effects of HLDP-SA nano-protectant on seed germination and growth. Seeds were sterilized in 0.1 % sodium hypochlorite solution, washed with ddH2O, soaked in HLDP-SA nano-protectant (SA concentration: 1, 2.5 and 5 mg/mL) and ddH2O (control) for 30 min, and cultured for 7 d at 28 °C. The germination rate was then calculated. Each group included 25 seeds, and the experiment was repeated three times. The long-term effects of HLDP-SA nano-protectant on seedling growth were further evaluated using the treated seeds, and the height of seedlings was recorded at 14 d after the germination. Each treatment was repeated five times.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Data analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The SPSS software (version 23.0, USA) was used to analyze the data. Descriptive statistics were represented by the mean and standard error. The data were analyzed using one-way ANOVA, and the Tukey test or independent t test was used to determine the difference between three or two groups at P<0.05 significance level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Results and discussion
Characterization and properties of commercial and synthesized nanocarriers
ce:section.ce:para: All nanocarriers were primarily consisted of stable spherical particles, and the particle sizes of MSN, Fe3O4, PLGA, CQD, SPc and HLDP nanocarriers were 49(±2), 200(±3), 8.6(±0.1), 5.6(±0.2), 115(±3) and 63(±4) nm, respectively (Supplementary Fig. 2a). The zeta potentials of four commercial nanocarriers were negative (MSN: −6.1; Fe3O4:-24.1; PLGA: −6.0; CQD: −30.7 mV), while two novel nanocarriers (SPc: 35.1; HLDP: 31.5 mV) were positively-charged (F=3113.346, P<0.01) (Supplementary Fig. 2b). Notably, the contact angle of HLDP nanocarrier was the smallest on the cotton leaves, and its retention was the highest among tested nanocarriers (Supplementary Fig. 2c-d).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading efficiencies of nanocarriers and self-assembly mechanism of SA nano-protectants
ce:section.ce:para: The SA was quantified using high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) by the peak area at 306 nm. Its standard calibration curve was constructed, and the R2 of the standard curve was 0.998 (Supplementary Fig. 3a). The loading efficiency of HLDP toward SA was calculated to be 49.28(±0.44)%, which was the highest among tested nanocarriers (MSN: 23.26(±0.30)%, Fe3O4: 32.37(±0.22)%, PLGA: 28.99(±0.37)%, CQD: 24.75(±0.24)%, SPc: 25.79(±0.07)%) (Supplementary Fig. 3b).

The dominant interaction force for the self-assembly of SPc-SA and HLDP-SA nano-protectants was analyzed by ITC. As shown in Supplementary Figs. 4–5, the high affinity constant Ka and low dissociation constant Kd indicated the strong interaction between nanocarrier and SA, and the negative ΔG value suggested that the interaction was spontaneous. The negative values of ΔH (−668.5 kJ/mol) and ΔS (−2139 J/mol·K) suggested that the self-assembly of SPc-SA nano-protectant was mainly driven by hydrogen bonding and Van der Waals force (Supplementary Fig. 4). Whereas, the negative value of ΔH (−21.18 kJ/mol) and positive value of ΔS (47.62 J/mol·K) indicated that the HLDP assembled with SA via electrostatic interaction (Supplementary Fig. 5). MSN and Fe3O4 have regular pore structures that can load drugs, and the cavities in CQD can also carry drugs [40–42]. Whereas, the functional groups in nanocarriers such as PLGA, SPc and HLDP can interact with drugs through hydrogen bonding, ionic bonding, electrostatic interaction, hydrophobic interaction, etc. [43]. Thus, each nanocarrier could remarkably affect the physical and chemical properties of SA nano-protectants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of SA nano-protectants
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 1 a, the particle sizes of MSN-SA, Fe3O4-SA, PLGA-SA, CQD-SA, SPc-SA and HLDP-SA nano-protectants were 121(±9), 112(±3), 9(±0.3), 6(±0.4), 94(±4) and 67(±3) nm, respectively. Based on the representative SEM and TEM images, the SA self-aggregated into needle-like particles, whereas all prepared SA nano-protectants were composed of nearly spherical particles with uniform size. Meanwhile, the zeta potentials of all SA nano-protectants were higher than those of corresponding nanocarriers, which was attributed to the positive charge of SA (Fig. 1b and Supplementary Fig. 2b) [44]. Among all prepared SA nano-protectants, the zeta potential of HLDP-SA nano-protectant (49.97 mV) was the highest, and that of CQD-SA nano-protectant (−15.37 mV) was the lowest (F=235.641, P<0.01). In addition, the shelf lives of all SA nano-protectants were relatively long with stable particle size and zeta potential after 120 d of storage at room temperature (Fig. 1c). Particle dissolution and release play a significant role in determining the properties and potential applications of nanomaterials [45–47]. The complex dissolution and release of SA from SA nano-protectants were also investigated (Supplementary Fig. 6). The particle sizes of SA nano-protectants were stable, thus their dissolution rates were slow. Furthermore, except for MSN-SA and Fe3O4-SA nano-protectants, other nano-protectants did not exhibit the capability of slow release. The particle size and surface charge of SA nano-protectants were directly related with the plant uptake and translocation, which were investigated in the following experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reduced contact angle, increased retention and improved plant uptake of SA nano-protectants
ce:section.ce:para: The contact angle of fungicide droplets can reflect the wettability of leaf surfaces, which is an important factor for fungicide retention. The contact angle of HLDP nano-protectant was 37.8(±1.1)° on cotton leaves, which was smaller than 68.4(±0.9)° of MSN-SA nano-protectant, 51.3(±0.7)° of Fe3O4-SA nano-protectant, 48.5(±0.4)° of PLGA-SA nano-protectant, 44.8(±0.4)° of CQD-SA nano-protectant, and 38.3(±0.1)° of SPc-SA nano-protectant (Fig. 1d). The retention pattern of SA nano-protectants was almostexactlyopposite to their contact angles, and the retention of HLDP-SA nano-protectant was the highest with 16.8(±0.3) mg/cm2 (F=28.278, P<0.01) (Fig. 1e). As expected, above patterns of contact angle and retention were similar to those of nanocarrier alone (Supplementary Fig. 2c-d). With the help of HLDP, the contact angle of SA decreased by 6.8°, and its retention increased by 4.1 mg/cm2, indicating that the HLDP had the potential to reduce the surface tension of fungicide droplets, promote their diffusion and adhesion, and thus increase the retention.

Effective deposition and strong adhesion to leaf surfaces are crucial for minimizing fungicide loss and improving plant uptake. Linearity of LC-MS experimental results (R2) was 0.998. Our data demonstrated that all nanocarriers could promote the plant uptake of SA, and the SA content was the highest in the roots, stems and leaves of cotton seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant (Fig. 1f). More specifically, the SA contents were 4.98(±0.09), 3.41(±0.01) and 6.41(±0.04) μg/mg, respectively in the roots, stems and leaves of seedlings treated with SA, and those remarkably increased to 16.62, 13.48 and 20.42 μg/mg with the aid of HLDP. The HLDP-mediated plant uptake of SA significantly increased by 3.34, 3.95 and 3.19 times. In summary, each nanocarrier could remarkably affect the physical and chemical properties of SA nano-protectants, and HLDP-SA nano-protectant exhibited the best adhesion and the strongest plant uptake. The adhesion and plant uptake of nano-protectants are related with the fungicidal activity, which are dependent on their basic properties, such as particle size, surface charge, etc. For instance, the nanoparticles with smaller sizes and positive charges display stronger adhesion to leaf surface and cell membrane [48,49]. In the current study, the contact angle of HLDP-SA nano-protectant was the smallest on the cotton leaves, and its retention was the highest among prepared SA nano-protectants, which facilitated the spreading of HLDP-SA nano-protectant on the leaf surface. Thus, HLDP-SA nano-protectant exhibited the best adhesion to cotton leaves and the strongest plant uptake. Notably, the particle size of HLDP-SA nano-protectant was not the smallest among SA nano-protectants, and its high plant uptake might be due to the positive charges on the surface. Previous studies have indicated that the complexation with positively-charged SPc can reduce the contact angle of pesticides on leaf surface, which improves the absorption and diffusion of pesticides [23,50]. Tebuconazole nano-fungicides with reduced contact angle possess the higher utilization efficiency than conventional tebuconazole [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced fungicidal activity of SA nano-protectants toward v. Dahliae
ce:section.ce:para: The V. dahliae strain (V991) was employed to evaluate the direct fungicidal activities of SA nano-protectants. The spore suspension was individually incubated with SA nano-protectants, and the spore germination rates were calculated to be 31.1(±2.5)% for SA alone, 20.6(±3.3)% for MSN-SA nano-protectant, 12.9(±2.7)% for Fe3O4-SA nano-protectant, 15.4(±3.6)% for PLGA-SA nano-protectant, 18.1(±3.5)% for CQD-SA nano-protectant, 3.1(±1.4)% for SPc-SA nano-protectant, and 2.0(±0.9)% for HLDP-SA nano-protectant after 12 h incubation, respectively (Fig. 2 a). The germination rate of spore incubated with SA alone for 24 h increased to 90.4(±0.9)%, whereas those with SA nano-protectants did not significantly alter (F=723.215, P<0.01).

The adverse impacts of SA nano-protectants on the growth of V991 strain were also examined. Compared with SA alone, all SA nano-protectants could suppress the growth of V991 strain, and the colony diameters were 7.4(±0.04), 4.79(±0.01), 6.34(±0.03), 6.57(±0.03), 4.34(±0.01) and 2.64(±0.03) cm for MSN-SA, Fe3O4-SA, PLGA-SA, CQD-SA, SPc-SA and HLDP-SA nano-protectants, respectively (Fig. 2b). However, the direct fungicidal activities of SA nano-protectants were mainly derived from nanocarriers themselves (Supplementary Fig. 7). All nanocarriers could suppress the spore germination of V991 strain, and its colony diameters were significantly decreased after the treatment with SPc or HLDP (F=2460.797, P<0.01). The characteristics of nano-protectants are related with the direct fungicidal activity. The positively-charged nano-protectants are more likely adhered to the surface of harmful organisms, and the smaller particle size can amplify the contact area of nano-protectants [10,12,24,52–54]. Our results demonstrated that the HLDP-SA nano-protectant with the highest zeta potential exhibited the strongest fungicidal activity toward V. dahliae. Notably, the particle sizes of PLGA-SA and CQD-SA nano-protectants were smaller than that of HLDP-SA nano-protectant, but their fungicidal activities were not higher, which might be related with their surface charge.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amplified plant defense induced by SA nano-protectants toward Verticillium wilt
ce:section.ce:para: Various SA nano-protectants were individually sprayed onto cotton seedlings, and root injury inoculation method was then adopted to examine the control effects on Verticillium wilt. As shown in Fig. 3 a, the infection symptom of seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant was the slightest. The disease index of seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant was 50.5(±1.0), which was the smallest among those treated with SA nano-protectants, including MSN-SA (65.4 ± 0.2), F3O4-SA (64.4 ± 0.2), PLGA-SA (73.6 ± 0.7), COQ-SA (74.9 ± 0.2) and SPc-SA nano-protectants (60.8 ± 0.3). Meanwhile, the relative fungal biomass of V991 strain treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant decreased to 0.5(±0.01) and the control effects of other SA nano-protectants were not as good as that of HLDP-SA nano-protectant (F=446.747, P<0.01).

Some other parameters were also employed to evaluate the disease severity of cotton seedlings. As the final decomposition product of lipid peroxidation, MDA reflects the rate and intensity of lipid peroxidation, and also indirectly reflects the degree of tissue peroxidative damage. The MDA content in the seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant was 8.3(±0.2) nmol/g FW, which was the lowest among SA nano-protectant-treated seedlings (F=4171.860, P<0.01) (Fig. 3b). As expected, the activities of SOD and CAT were significantly higher in the seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant than most other SA nano-protectants, which increased by 115 % and 256 %, respectively compared to the seedlings treated with SA alone (F=477.018, P<0.01). However, the POD activity was not significantly variable in seedlings treated with various SA nano-protectants. In addition, proline and soluble protein that are important osmotic regulators, generally increased in the seedlings treated with SA nano-protectants (Fig. 3c). The contents of proline and soluble protein were 77.32(±2.06) and 1.81(±1.49) mg/g FW, respectively in the seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant, which were the highest among the seedlings treated with SA nano-protectants (F=116.650, P<0.01). The control effects of SA on Verticillium wilt were not ideal possibly due to the application concentration and time of SA [55].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Enhanced fungicidal activity of HLDP-SA nano-protectant via inhibiting metabolism and weakening pathogenicity of v. Dahliae
ce:section.ce:para: Based on above results, the HLDP-SA nano-protectant with the best control effect and SA alone were employed to treat V991 strain, and its mycelia were harvested for RNA-seq to elucidate the enhanced fungicidal activity of HLDP-SA nano-protectant. The sequencing quality and correlation within biological replicates were relatively high for the following analysis (Supplementary Table 2 and Supplementary Fig. 8). Compared to SA alone, a total of 1756 up-regulated and 1684 down-regulated differentially expressed genes (DEGs) were identified in the V991 strain treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant (Fig. 4 a). The DEGs were classified into various gene pathways, including the transmembrane transporter activity, phosphate ion transport, intrinsic component of membrane, starch and sucrose metabolism, steroid biosynthesis, glycerophospholipid metabolism pathways, etc. (Fig. 4b-c).

Compared to SA alone, the HLDP-SA nano-protectant up-regulated some crucial genes of V991 strain involved in intracellular transport pathway (Fig. 4d). More specifically, the genes encoding the vacuolar protein sorting-associated protein (VDAG_01543 and VDAG_10002) and SNF7 family protein (VDAG_09167) were up-regulated by 3.77, 3.93 and 6.43-folds, respectively. These data revealed that the HLDP-SA nano-protectant accelerated the translocation of exogenous substances across the cell membrane, which might lead to the membrane damage of V991 strain. The VDAG_01543 exhibits high structural and functional similarity with Vacuolar protein-sorting-associated protein 25 (VPS25), which plays a crucial role in the formation, transport and release of vesicles within cells [56]. The VDAG_09167 belongs to the SNF7 family, a group of proteins that regulate cellular metabolism, transport proteins and nucleic acids [57].

The conversion of glucose to acetone is one of the key steps in initiating the tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle. Our results demonstrated that the genes involved in this pathway, including VDAG_06513 (6-phosphogluconate esterase), VDAG_07446 (phosphoenolpyruvate carboxyl kinase) and VDAG_08259 (malate dehydrogenase) were down-regulated in V991 strain treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant, indicating that the HLDP-SA nano-protectant led to the reduction in pyruvate synthesis and the supply of TCA cycle precursor (acetyl CoA) to suppress the fungal metabolism [58].

Furthermore, as positive regulators in the pathogenicity of V. dahliae, three secreted protein encoding genes VDAG_08721, VDAG_02735 and VDAG_09343 were down-regulated in V991 strain treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant, and their down-regulation might reduce the virulence or loss of pathogenicity in V. dahliae in the current study [59]. The qRT-PCR results were consistent with the transcriptome findings, further validating the gene expression changes (Supplementary Fig. 9). Herein, the direct fungicidal activity of HLDP-SA nano-protectant might be derived from the membrane damage, hyphal metabolism inhibition and pathogenicity reduction.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Amplified plant defense induced by HLDP-SA nano-protectant via activating the biosynthesis of plant secondary metabolites
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the mechanism of amplified plant defense against Verticillium wilt, the metabolome sequencing was carried out to analyze the differentially accumulated metabolites (DAMs) in cotton seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant and SA alone. Principal component analysis (PCA) was used to test the correlation of replicate samples, and two principal components accounted for 31.7 % and 18.0 % of the variance, indicating good and reliable experimental reproducibility (Supplementary Fig. 10). As shown in Fig. 5 a, Supplementary Table 3 and Supplementary Fig. 11, a total of 121 DAMs were identified in HLDP-SA nano-protectant treatment, of which 50 were up-regulated, accounting for 41.32 %, and 71 were down-regulated, accounting for 58.68 %. The 50 up-regulated DAMs included SA, ABA, gossypol, naringenin and vanillic acid, suggesting that HLDP-SA nano-protectant might induce the production of endogenous plant hormones, prenol lipids and organic acids to amplify the plant immune responses. Similarly, KEGG analysis also indicated that the DAMs were primarily enriched in lysine biosynthesis, biosynthesis of various plant secondary metabolites, plant hormone signal translation, isoflavonoid biosynthesis, arginine and proline metabolism, tyrosine metabolism, etc., which indicated that the HLDP-SA nano-protectant activated the plant defense via regulating the synthesis of plant hormones and secondary metabolites (Fig. 5b).

Cotton seedlings were also harvested for RNA-seq, and the sequencing quality was good for the following analysis (Supplementary Table 3). Compared to SA alone, a total of 896 up-regulated and 323 down-regulated DEGs were identified in the cotton seeding treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant (Supplementary Fig. 12a). The DEGs were classified into three categories, including the cellular process, metabolic process, cell part, organelle, binding, catalytic activity, etc. (Supplementary Fig. 12b). Compared to SA alone, the HLDP-SA nano-protectant up-regulated some critical genes involved in intracellular delivery and defense response (Supplementary Fig. 12c). The conjoint analysis of the metabolomics and transcriptomics was carried out to identify 43 common pathways, including plant hormone signal transduction, arginine and proline metabolism, tyrosine metabolism, tryptophan metabolism, terpenoid backbone biosynthesis, etc. (Fig. 5c).

As an important plant hormone, SA participates in both biological and abiotic stress responses, and its biosynthesis was firstly analyzed (Fig. 5d). Compared to SA treatment, four SA biosynthesis-related genes aroC, ICS, PAL and EDS5 were up-regulated in HLDP-SA nano-protectant treatment, and some crucial substances for SA biosynthesis such as isochorismate, phenylalanine, benzoic acid, and SA were all up-regulated, indicating the activation of SA biosynthesis via HLDP-SA nano-protectant. There are two main pathways involved in the synthesis of SA in plants: the isochorismate synthase (ICS) pathway and phenylalanine ammonia-lyase (PAL) pathway [26,29]. Based on the current study, the application of HLDP-SA nano-protectant further activated both ICS and PAL pathways for SA synthesis, which contributed to the enhanced plant resistance against biotic and abiotic stresses. Similarly, exogenous application of SA can activate the ICS pathway to establish systemic acquired resistance in Arabidopsis, resulting in resistance to a variety of pathogens [60].

Meanwhile, the ABA biosynthesis was also activated by HLDP-SA nano-protectant, and genes NCED and ZEP, and vital substances xanthoxin, abscisic aldehyde and ABA were all up-regulated (Fig. 5e). As a unique protectant in cotton plants, gossypol is closely related to systemic resistance to Verticillium wilt. ABA is another important plant hormone involved in plant defense responses [61]. The DEGs and DAMs participating in the ABA synthesis pathway were commonly up-regulated in seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant, which was consistent to a previous publication that the exogenous application of SA can significantly increase the accumulation level of ABA within rice [62].

The DEGs and DAMs in mevalonic acid and gossypol synthesis pathways are shown in Fig. 5f. Crucial enzymes encoded by (+)-δ-glycine synthetase (+)-δ-cadinene synthase (CDNS), CYP82D113 and 2-oxoglutarate/Fe (II)-dependent dioxygenase (2-ODD-1) for gossypol synthesis were up-regulated, and the gossypol amount was also increased in the seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant. Gossypol was a vital phytoalexin with antifungal and insecticidal properties [63]. Based on the current results, the application of HLDP-SA nano-protectant could accelerate the biosynthesis of plant secondary metabolites, which amplified the plant defense against the infection with V. dahliae. To verify the results of metabolomics and transcriptomics, the biosynthesis of SA, ABA and gossypol was further examined via HLPC. The R2 of the ABA standard curve was 0.996, and that of gossypol was 0.999. Their contents in the seedlings treated with HLDP-SA nano-protectant were 2.03, 2.87 and 1.79 times those treated with SA alone, confirming that the HLDP-SA nano-protectant activated the biosynthesis of SA, ABA and gossypol (Supplementary Fig. 13).

ce:section.ce:section-title: No adverse impacts of HLDP-SA nano-protectant on plants at working concentration
ce:section.ce:para: The potential negative impacts of HLDP-SA nano-protectant were evaluated toward cotton and rice seeds. As shown in Supplementary Fig. 14, HLDP-SA nano-protectant exhibited negligible toxicity toward seeds at the working concentration (1 mg/mL), with no significant influences on seed germination or seedling growth. However, the seed germination and seedling growth were significantly affected by the application of HLDP-SA nano-protectant at high concentrations (2.5 and 5 mg/mL). The mechanism underling the direct toxicity of nanocarriers involves the cell membrane damage, nucleus disintegration, cytoplasmic efflux, oxidative stress and interference of intracellular signaling [64,65]. Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are considered as a common cause of nanocarrier-induced toxicity [66,67].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: In this work, we constructed a series of SA nano-protectants via different nanomaterials, and evaluated the best nanomaterial for plant protection from two aspects: the direct biotoxicity of introduced nanomaterial and nanomaterial-mediated enhanced delivery of AIs. As shown in Supplementary Fig. 15, the HLDP could self-assemble with SA via electrostatic interaction into HLDP-SA nano-protectant with uniform size. On the one hand, the HLDP-SA nano-protectant could activate the delivery and translocation of exogenous substances into V. dahliae, and inhibit its metabolism and weaken its pathogenicity, which exhibited the highest fungicidal activity. On the other hand, the HLDP-SA nano-protectant displayed better adhesion performance on cotton leaves with enhanced plant uptake, which amplified the plant defense induced by SA via accelerating the biosynthesis of plant secondary metabolites. Thus, the HLDP did not only act as an efficient nanocarrier/adjuvant for SA delivery, but also showed direct fungicidal activity toward V. dahliae. It is the first success to compare the direct fungicidal activity and delivery efficiency of nanocarriers, and the dual role of HLDP as adjuvant and AI provided a powerful nanomaterial for designing nano-protectant with high fungicidal activity and amplified plant defense.
